title: How Clearing Forests Can Change the Rain They Create

lesson.learn:
Forests do more than store carbon. They also recycle moisture into the atmosphere through evapotranspiration, which helps build clouds and support rainfall. When forests are removed, the land often becomes hotter and drier, and the local water cycle can weaken. In some regions, that can reduce rainfall far beyond the cleared area.

This is why deforestation is not just a land-use issue. It is a climate and hydrology issue. Trees help create the conditions that keep forests rainy in the first place.
lesson.simpler: Trees put moisture back into the air. When forests are cut down, less water rises into the atmosphere and rainfall can drop.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Deforestation can reduce rainfall because forests help pump water vapor into the air.
lesson.deeperDive:
A forest acts like a biological water pump. Leaves release water vapor, roots move water from soil, and the canopy shades the ground so moisture lasts longer. Large-scale clearing breaks that system. With less evapotranspiration, fewer clouds may form and dry conditions can spread. The effect can be especially strong where rainfall already depends on forest moisture recycling.

This matters in the Amazon, the Congo Basin, Southeast Asia, and other forested regions. The link between trees and rain shows that cutting forests can create feedback loops: less forest means less rain, and less rain makes it harder for forest to return.
lesson.funFact: Some rainforests make a big share of their own rain.
references: NASA Earth Observatory - forests and rainfall
FAO - forests and the water cycle

people: Forest ecologists - scientists who study water recycling
Climate modelers - researchers who link land use and rainfall
Indigenous land stewards - communities who manage forests sustainably
Conservation planners - people working to protect forest cover
places: The Amazon Basin - major rainfall recycling region
The Congo Basin - another key forest climate system
Southeast Asian rainforests - areas sensitive to clearing
Watersheds and catchments - where land cover affects water flow
years: 1980s - forest-climate coupling becomes a major research topic
2020s - deforestation and climate feedbacks remain a major concern
concepts: Evapotranspiration - water released from plants into the air
Moisture recycling - rainwater returning to the atmosphere
Feedback loop - a change that reinforces itself
Watershed - land that drains into a common water system
